did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a hybrid fused deep learning framework integrating ResNet50, MobileNetV3-S, and EfficientNetV2-S for skin cancer classification. The study was conducted on the HAM10000 dataset containing dermatoscopic images of seven skin lesion categories, utilizing AI models to achieve classification results.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving a proposed hybrid fused deep learning framework (integrating ResNet50, MobileNetV3-S, and EfficientNetV2-S) for skin cancer classification. It details specific methodology including dataset preparation (HAM10000), model architecture, training configuration, and reports original quantitative results (e.g., validation accuracy of 85.33%, precision, recall, F1-scores) and confusion matrix analysis. Although published in a conference proceedings (ICMNWC), it contains original research results rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Hybrid Fused Deep Learning Framework using EfficientNetV2-S, MobileNetV3-S and ResNet50 for Skin Cancer Classification
journal: 2025 5th International Conference on Mobile Networks and Wireless Communications (ICMNWC)
brief_summary: This study proposes a hybrid fused deep learning framework integrating ResNet50, MobileNetV3-Small, and EfficientNetV2-Small to address challenges in skin cancer classification, such as intra-class similarity and class imbalance. The model was evaluated on the HAM10000 dataset, which contains 10,015 dermatoscopic images across seven skin lesion categories, with images resized to 224x224x3 and class weights applied during training. The experimental evaluation utilized an 80:10:10 stratified train-validation-test split, repeated over five independent runs to ensure consistency. The proposed framework achieved a validation accuracy of 85.33%, with precision, recall, and F1-scores of 86.1%, 85.32%, and 85.6%, respectively. Comparative analysis demonstrated that the hybrid model improved the macro-F1 score by 13.9% over the strongest individual network baselines. These results indicate that the feature-level fusion of multiple architectures enhances sensitivity and specificity, offering strong potential for clinical decision support in dermatology.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the seven diagnostic classes in the HAM10000 dataset used for classification. Identified as a minority class with strong recognition performance.

all_task_types.target: Seven distinct skin lesion categories (dermatofibroma, vascular lesions, actinic keratoses and intraepithelial carcinoma, basal cell carcinoma, melanocytic nevi, melanoma, and benign keratosis-like lesions)
all_task_types.details: The paper performs multiclass classification of skin lesions using the HAM10000 dataset. The model outputs discrete diagnostic categories via a softmax layer. Performance is evaluated using accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-scores, with a confusion matrix analyzing per-class classification performance.

datasets_used.name: HAM10000 (Human Against Machine with 10,000 training images)
datasets_used.description: A public dataset containing 10,015 high-resolution dermatoscopic images of skin lesions. The images were originally at 600x450 pixels and resized to 224x224x3 for the study.

use.training: Used for training the hybrid fused deep learning framework (ResNet50, MobileNetV3-S, EfficientNetV2-S) with class weights applied to address class imbalance.
use.validation: Used for validation to assess model performance (accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score) during the 25-epoch training process.
use.testing: Used for final testing and evaluation of the model's classification performance.
use.other: The dataset was split using a stratified data-frame-based split with a fixed random state of 42. Experiments were repeated over five independent runs to confirm consistency.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To divide the dataset into training, validation, and testing subsets while preserving class distribution.
To confirm consistency of results by repeating experiments five times.
To address class imbalance during the training phase.
To calculate overall performance metrics by averaging across all classes.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using descriptive statistics (accuracy, precision, recall, F1-scores) and comparative analysis against baseline models without statistical significance testing.

HAM10000.experiment: Hybrid Fused Deep Learning Framework (ResNet50 + MobileNetV3-S + EfficientNetV2-S)
HAM10000.notes: The hybrid model achieved a 13.9% improvement in macro-F1 score over the strongest individual network baseline (ResNet50). The model was evaluated over five independent runs. Confusion matrix analysis indicated strong recognition for dominant categories (NV, BKL) and reasonably strong recognition for minority classes (DF).

architecture_used.main_model: Hybrid Fused Deep Learning Framework (ResNet50 + MobileNetV3-Small + EfficientNetV2-Small)
architecture_used.pretraining: ImageNet pre-trained weights (weights='imagenet') for all three backbone networks

backbones.name: EfficientNetV2-Small
backbones.details: Uses Fused-MBConv and Squeeze-and-Excitation (SE) blocks with h-swish activation. Ends with GlobalAveragePooling2D producing Feature Vector A (dimension 1280).

backbones.name: MobileNetV3-Small
backbones.details: Uses bottleneck blocks with expansion, depthwise, and projection layers. Ends with GlobalAveragePooling2D producing Feature Vector B (dimension 576).

backbones.name: ResNet50
backbones.details: Uses residual connections with identity and convolutional block processing across four stages. Ends with GlobalAveragePooling2D producing Feature Vector C (dimension 2048).

architecture_details.fusion_mechanism: Feature-level fusion via concatenation of feature vectors A, B, and C. The concatenated fused vector F has a total dimensionality of 3904 (1280 + 576 + 2048).

regularization: Class weights to address class imbalance

classification_or_output_head.output: Probability distribution across seven skin lesion categories

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: Hybrid Fused Deep Learning Framework (ResNet50 + MobileNetV3-Small + EfficientNetV2-Small)
preprocessing_techniques: Image resizing: Images originally at 600x450 pixels were resized to 224x224x3 to maintain uniform input dimensions across all models.
Stratified data splitting: The dataset was divided into 80% training, 10% validation, and 10% testing subsets using a stratified data-frame-based split to preserve class distribution.
Shuffling: Stratification and shuffling were performed with a fixed random state of 42 for reproducibility.
Class weight calculation: Class weights were applied during training to address class imbalance, calculated using the formula involving total samples, samples per class, and total classes.
conclusions: The proposed hybrid fused deep learning framework, integrating ResNet50, MobileNetV3-Small, and EfficientNetV2-Small, effectively addresses challenges in skin lesion classification such as intra-class similarity, inter-class variations, and severe class imbalance.

Feature-level fusion of multiple architectures yields superior sensitivity and specificity compared to standalone models, providing strong evidence that the hybrid approach improves the accuracy and reliability of skin lesion diagnosis.
The hybrid model achieved a validation accuracy of 85.33%, with precision, recall, and F1-scores of 86.1%, 85.32%, and 85.6% respectively, demonstrating robust performance on the HAM10000 dataset.
The hybrid model demonstrated a significant performance gain, improving the macro-F1 score by 13.9% over the strongest individual network baseline (ResNet50, which achieved a macro-F1 of 71.65%).

The model showed strong recognition for dominant categories such as melanocytic nevi (NV) and benign keratosis-like lesions (BKL), while maintaining reasonable accuracy on minority classes including dermatofibroma.

The study confirms the potential of AI-driven solutions for clinical decision support in dermatology, particularly in areas with limited access to dermatological expertise, by offering diagnostic accuracy levels comparable to skilled dermatologists.

While the hybrid approach enhances robustness and generalizability, the study acknowledges that issues such as overfitting, imbalanced datasets, and lack of explainability remain challenges hindering the broader clinical adoption of deep learning models.
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on the HAM10000 dataset, which is known for significant class imbalance and potential selection bias, limiting the generalizability of the results to other populations or imaging devices.
No external validation was performed; the model was only evaluated on the internal test split of the HAM10000 dataset, raising concerns about overfitting to this specific dataset's distribution.
The paper does not report formal statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values) for the reported performance improvements, relying instead on descriptive statistics and comparative analysis.

While the hybrid model improved macro-F1 by 13.9% over baselines, the absolute validation accuracy (85.33%) and recall (85.32%) remain moderate, indicating that diagnostic errors still occur, particularly for minority classes.

The authors acknowledge that issues such as lack of explainability (black-box nature) and overfitting remain challenges hindering broader clinical adoption, which are not addressed in the current work.
The dataset was resized from 600x450 to 224x224 pixels, which may result in the loss of fine-grained dermatological features critical for distinguishing between visually similar lesions.
The study was conducted by researchers from a single institution (HKBK College of Engineering) without mention of multi-center collaboration or diverse demographic representation in the data source.

The paper claims potential for clinical decision support but does not provide any evidence of clinical workflow integration, user study, or comparison with actual dermatologist performance on the same test set.
